blocks.text: Chapter 2.
How Money Warps Judgment

Second night with Dobelli's field guide, friend. Last night the traps came from other people — the crowd, the white coat, the gift, the smile. Tonight's traps need no one else at all. They live in your pocket.

Because here is the strange truth this chapter circles: money bends thinking the way gravity bends light. The same brain that counts perfectly well goes crooked the moment the numbers wear a currency sign. And the first trap is the most expensive one in the book — nations have flown into it at twice the speed of sound.
The airplane that everyone knew would fail

The Concorde was a marvel — Paris to New York in under four hours, a white dart outrunning the sun. And here is the part they don't put on the postcards: Britain and France knew early that it would never make money. Not suspected. Knew. Yet both governments kept funding it, year after year, decade after decade.
Pause and guess: They knew the numbers didn't work. So why did two sober governments keep pouring fortunes into a plane that could never pay them back?
Ready? Here is what happened.

Because of the money already spent. Stopping would have turned all those sunk millions into an official, public admission of waste — so they "protected" the old investment by burning new money on top of it. That is the sunk cost fallacy, and economists literally call irrational escalation the Concorde effect. The cold logic it violates is simple: money already spent is gone no matter what you do next — only future costs and future benefits can justify a decision. But we hate the admission more than we hate the loss, so we sit through the dreadful film because we paid for the ticket, hold the plunging stock "until it comes back to what I paid," stay years more in a dead relationship because of the years already given. Dobelli's test cuts through all of it in one sentence: knowing what I know now, would I start this today? If no — then the only rational reason to continue does not exist, and everything else you feel is just past investment, talking.
The crooked scale in your chest

Now the engine underneath — the finding at the heart of Kahneman and Tversky's Nobel-winning work. Imagine losing a hundred rupees this morning, then finding a hundred this evening. Mathematically you're even. Emotionally, friend, you are not — and everyone knows it.

Loss aversion: a loss weighs roughly twice as much as the same-sized gain. The scale in your chest is crooked, and evolution bent it on purpose — for your ancestors, one serious loss of food or standing could be fatal, while a missed gain was merely a pity. The cautious survived; we are their children.

Watch what the crooked scale does today. "You lose money by not insulating your house" persuades far better than "you save money by insulating" — logically identical, emotionally worlds apart, and every marketer knows it. Investors cling to losing stocks (selling makes the paper loss real) and dump winners too early (locking in the little pleasure). Employees bury mistakes and dodge bold projects, because a visible failure hurts twice what an equal success helps — and so whole companies drift into timidity nobody chose. You cannot straighten the scale. You can only stop trusting it: when a small feared loss is outvoting a larger expected gain, write both numbers down, and let the arithmetic decide instead of the flinch.
The mug that doubled in price

Kahneman ran a mischievous little market. He gave some students a coffee mug and asked what they'd sell it for. Other students — no mug — were asked what they'd pay for one. Same mug. Same room. Same afternoon.

Sellers demanded roughly double what buyers offered. Nothing had changed but ownership — and ownership alone doubled the mug's worth in its owner's eyes. That is the endowment effect: loss aversion wearing your possessions. Selling doesn't feel like a trade; it feels like an amputation, and we bill the buyer for the pain.

At Duke University, where basketball tickets go by lottery to students who camp out for days, Dan Ariely found the monster version: lottery losers would pay about $170 for a ticket; lottery winners wanted about $2,400 to part with the identical ticket. A fourteen-fold gap, made of nothing but "it's mine now." It's why homeowners overprice their houses with sentiment the buyer never sees, why the auction leader overbids for an item that already feels his — and why companies shower you with free trials and easy returns: once the thing sits in your home, giving it back registers as a loss, and most people quietly keep it.
Dobelli's counsel has a monk's flavor: hold your possessions as things the universe has lent you. And when you sell, price at what a stranger would pay — never at what the parting costs your heart.
The rats of Hanoi
Colonial Hanoi had a rat problem, so the French administrators did the obvious clever thing: a cash bounty for every rat tail delivered.
Pause and guess: Rational citizens, real money, clear target. What do you think happened to the rats of Hanoi?
Ready? Here is what happened.

The rat population grew. Citizens caught rats, snipped off their tails... and released them to breed — more rats meant more tails meant more money. The bounty had become a subsidy for rat farming. That is the incentive super-response tendency: people respond powerfully to incentives — but to the incentive as literally written, never to the intention behind it. And its quiet everyday form should change how you take advice: the hourly-billing lawyer has an incentive for the case to crawl; the commission-paid adviser, to churn your portfolio; the per-operation surgeon, to operate. None of them need be dishonest — the incentive bends honest judgment silently. Dobelli's rule: never ask a barber whether you need a haircut. Always ask what your adviser earns from your yes. And beware the backfire, too — motivation crowding: when Haifa daycares fined parents for late pickups, lateness increased, because the fine turned a moral duty into a cheap purchasable service — and when the fine was removed, the courtesy never came back. Money can buy the favor and kill the friendship's logic in the same transaction. A small payment is often worse than none.
The house's money is your money

One more warp, and it explains every casino floor on earth. A gambler up a thousand feels he's playing with "the house's money" — so he bets it wildly, and losing it "doesn't count." But every rupee has identical purchasing power, friend. The house-money effect — Richard Thaler proved it in the lab — makes windfalls feel less real than wages: bonuses, refunds, cashback, inheritance, "free" credit, all spent faster and dumber than money we sweated for. Lottery winners going broke is this bias at life scale.
The fix is to refuse the two mental accounts: when a windfall arrives, dress it in work clothes — move it into the same account as your salary and let it cool for thirty days before touching a cent.

And the last illusion: the hedonic treadmill. We chase the raise, the bigger house, the faster car, expecting lasting joy — and adapt within months, sliding back to baseline; studies find lottery winners near their old happiness in about ninety days. So spend where adaptation is weakest: avoid the negatives you never get used to — commutes, noise, chronic stress — and buy free time, autonomy, and friendships, the few goods the treadmill cannot eat.
Before you go

Six traps tonight, one thread through them all: money is never just arithmetic in your head — it arrives wearing feelings. Old spending demands loyalty. Losses scream twice as loud as gains. Ownership doubles prices. Incentives rewrite behavior letter by letter, and windfalls whisper I don't count. The defense, every time, is to strip the feeling off the number and look again.

But tomorrow, friend, we leave money for something your brain handles even worse. It has no organ for it at all — a blindness so complete that a plane crash on the news will reshape your fears for a month while the statistics that actually predict your future sit unread. Chance. Probability. The mathematics of maybe.
Next time: why your gut cannot count — and the famous puzzle about a bank teller named Linda that fools nearly everyone who meets it.
Key takeaways
Sunk costs: money already spent is gone — ask only "would I start this today?" and exit if the answer is no.
Loss aversion: losses weigh double — when a feared small loss outvotes a bigger expected gain, let arithmetic decide, not the flinch.
Endowment effect: ownership inflates value — sell at the price you'd pay as a stranger.
Incentives rule behavior as written, not as intended — never ask a barber if you need a haircut; ask what your adviser gains.
Small payments can destroy goodwill permanently (motivation crowding) — sometimes no incentive beats a cheap one.
House money: windfalls spend like play money — route every bonus into work clothes and a 30-day cooling period; buy time and friendships, not treadmill upgrades.
